commonRoofingChallenges: Period-appropriate material recommendations for Victorian and Craftsman homes (slate alternatives, designer asphalt mimicking historic profiles)
Tight downtown blocks limiting dumpster and equipment placement
Dust and noise mitigation expected on dense walkable streets
Older homes with original decking that often needs partial replacement
uniqueAngleElaboration: Kirkwood is one of the St. Louis metro's most distinctive historic communities — a walkable downtown anchored by the 19th-century Kirkwood Train Station, surrounded by streets of Victorian, Craftsman, and Foursquare homes that have been continuously lived in since the late 1800s. Roofing in Kirkwood has to respect that. Period-appropriate material choices matter: a Queen Anne Victorian doesn't take a 3-tab asphalt shingle, and a Craftsman bungalow looks wrong with the wrong shingle profile. Modern designer asphalt lines, synthetic slate, and where budget allows real slate or cedar shake are common recommendations. Kirkwood's tight residential blocks add logistics challenges: dumpster placement has to consider neighbor driveways, equipment staging has to work around walkability, and dust and noise mitigation matters more here than on a half-acre lot in O'Fallon. Older Kirkwood homes also tend to need partial decking replacement during tear-off — original 1x6 plank decking from the early 1900s often hides moisture damage that doesn't appear until the shingles come off.
sourcesConsulted: General knowledge of historic-walkable St. Louis County housing
